summary: DHH reflects on the collapse of Caput, a company where he worked for about six months. He describes the experience as a crash course in bad software management and poor leadership. The company's press release blamed tough financial conditions, but DHH argues the real cause was incompetent management. He frames the painful experience as ultimately valuable, having taught him lasting lessons about how not to lead.
key_points: Direct experience with bad management teaches more than any book on the subject
Failing companies almost always blame external conditions rather than acknowledging internal leadership failures
Painful professional experiences can be reframed as valuable if the lessons stick
Learning requires humility — leaders who refuse to accept responsibility are doomed to repeat mistakes
money_quotes: Caput taught me more depressing truths about (at times clueless) software management and (consistently clueless) leadership in the around six months I spend there than all my litterature on the subjects ever did.
There's no better way of learning how to lead than to have the classic mistakes burned into your fabric like that.
But I'm not bitter, I'm grateful!
In classic New Economy-style, the press release cites bad financial weathers as the main cause of company deconstruction. It of course couldn't have anything to do with how the company was runned.
If you truely do believe that all your woes are do to a tough financial markets, I pity your future colleagues (or subordinates).
key_insight: Companies that blame market conditions for their failure instead of examining their own leadership are guaranteed to repeat those failures.
